← the Atlas

hcom vs. kolu — what to adopt

Comparisons·seedling·proposed·JUL 01, 2026

hcom hooks coding agents together through tool hooks and a shared SQLite event log, giving each agent an addressable inbox, an event feed, and subscriptions — the one thing kolu has no equivalent for. kolu's agent-drives-agent story today is screen-scraping (type keystrokes into a PTY, read the reply off the screen). The borrow is hcom's channel — an agent message surface built on the awareness surface kolu already ships; the thing we keep is kolu's substrate — first-party PTYs and typed surface-over-ssh, not a shared-PSK MQTT relay.

Contents

  • Two bets on the same problem
  • What to adopt — an agent message surface on pulam
  • /debate — the first consumer that stops screen-scraping
  • Where kolu keeps its own bet
  • The smallest first step

A read of hcom (a single Rust binary that “hooks your coding agents together” — message, watch, and spawn each other across terminals) held against what kolu already is. Claims checked against both codebases. hcom and kolu chase the same goal — several coding agents working together across terminals — and bet on opposite substrates to get there. That makes hcom a clean reference for the one capability kolu is missing, and a clean foil for the two bets kolu should keep.

Two bets on the same problem

hcom owns no terminal. It rides each tool’s hook system (Claude Code’s PostToolUse/Stop, Gemini, Codex, OpenCode, Cursor, …) and writes everything — messages, status changes, file edits, tool calls — into a local SQLite event log. That single move buys every agent a queryable identity: a name, a status (active/blocked/listening), an inbox, a live event feed, and a transcript. Agents then @mention each other, tag messages with an intent (request → always reply, inform → reply if useful, ack → don’t), isolate traffic into threads, and subscribe to events with a SQL predicate that auto-sends a message when it fires (hcom events sub … --on-hit-text …). Delivery is a hook injecting the message mid-turn between tool calls, or a wake that nudges an idle agent. Collision detection ships on by default: two agents editing the same file within 30 s both get pinged. It’s a coordination fabric — agents talk in a channel.

kolu made the other bet, and made it well. It owns the PTYs first-party: kaval is a daemon that holds every terminal and outlives its clients; pulam dials kaval and derives per-terminal awareness — git repo/branch/dirtiness, the branch’s PR and CI checks, the live agent and its state, the foreground process — serving it as one typed @kolu/surface collection that mirrors across machines over ssh. The agent-state feed is real and precise: a closed set {thinking, tool_use, running_background, awaiting_user, waiting} folded from a memoryless producer (see awareness as a fold). kolu has a superb substrate — but no channel on top of it.

So how does one kolu agent drive another today? It screen-scrapes. The kolu skill is a create → send → snapshot loop: spawn the inner agent in a kaval PTY, kaval-tui send the prompt as keystrokes, a second send --key Enter to submit, kaval-tui wait --until idle:<ms> for the turn to go quiet, then kaval-tui snapshot --viewport and read the reply off the screen. The skill’s own warnings are the tell of how much tax that is: send is blind (it writes whether or not the agent is ready); an Enter sent in the same breath as the text races bracketed-paste and is silently dropped; and idle “≠ the reply is correct” — quiescence can’t tell “finished” from “blocked asking you a question,” so you must snapshot and guess. That’s the whole cost of coordinating through a screen instead of a channel: the message is bytes typed into a TUI and pixels read back out.

Not a competitor — a reference and a foil

hcom is the same first-mover bet as herdr in a different axis. herdr validated first-party PTYs (kolu’s substrate). hcom validates the layer above it — agents as addressable endpoints on a shared log. kolu has the first and lacks the second. The borrow writes itself.

What to adopt — an agent message surface on pulam

The borrow is not hcom’s implementation (a parallel SQLite database, a fresh hook harness per tool). kolu already has the primitives; the borrow is the shape. Build a message channel as a new capability on the surface pulam already serves — reusing the source of truth, not standing up a rival one.

  • Addressable inbox — a surface Collection, not a new database. pulam already keys awareness by terminal id and every terminal already carries the agent’s name. Add an inbox collection (and a messages Event) to that same surface. @name routing is a lookup over keys kolu already broadcasts. hcom needed SQLite because it owns no shared state; kolu owns a typed reactive one already — the mailbox is a few keys on it, mirrored across machines for free by mirrorRemoteSurface.

  • Subscriptions — “react when X.” hcom’s highest-leverage feature is events sub: a predicate plus an auto-sent message. kolu’s awareness is already an event stream folded over a log, and @kolu/surface ships an Event primitive. Expose a subscribe verb over it and the reactive topologies hcom’s scripts lean on fall out: “when @codex reaches awaiting_user, send it the next task”; “notify me if a file I touched is edited by another agent within 30 s” (collision detection — kolu already has the file-edit sensor, it just never cross-references agents).

  • An intent protocol (request / inform / ack). This is a pure convention and it directly retires the screen-scrape loop’s worst guess. Today kaval-tui wait --until idle fires whether the agent finished or is blocked asking you something, so the driver must snapshot and infer “is this a question?” An intent tag on the message makes the turn contract explicit — the driven agent knows whether a reply is owed — so the loop stops inferring conversational state from terminal quiescence.

  • Deliver as a prompt, not a keystroke. hcom injects a message between tool calls through the tool’s own hooks; kolu injects keystrokes into a PTY, which is exactly what races bracketed-paste. kolu already classifies agents per-adapter in @kolu/integrations/anyagent (Claude Code / Codex / OpenCode session watchers). Route delivery through that adapter as a prompt, and the blind-write / dropped-Enter failure mode is gone by construction — the message arrives as input the agent is ready to read, not bytes fired at a maybe-ready TUI.

  • Ship it as a skill + workflow scripts — the way hcom does. hcom’s reach comes from a ~700-token bootstrap primer plus hcom run debate|confess|ensemble bundled multi-agent scripts. kolu already has the delivery vehicles: the kolu skill, the debate skill, and the Workflow tool. The message surface is what turns those from screen-scrape choreography into a channel the scripts address directly — a kolu-tui msg @name verb the skill teaches, worker-reviewer / ensemble / hub-spoke topologies the workflow scripts compose.

/debate — the first consumer that stops screen-scraping

The /debate skill is the sharpest proof, because it is built directly on the screen-scrape loop and inherits every one of its failure modes. Its handoff is literally “after taking a turn, ping the other terminal with kaval-tui send so it knows to proceed,” and it deliberately bans done-signal polling — “do not use pulam-tui… the agents prompt each other directly with kaval-tui send.” A debate is nothing but turn-taking between agents on terminals, so it is exactly the workload a channel is for. Three concrete wins, each tied to a mechanic the skill already describes:

  • The handoff is the intent protocol. “Ping the other terminal so it knows to proceed” is --intent request; ending the round is ack. Today that ping is a blind keystroke nudge that races bracketed-paste (the loop’s #1 failure) and has to be confirmed with a snapshot. As an addressed kolu-tui msg @opponent --intent request it lands reliably, and the turn contract is explicit instead of inferred from terminal quiescence.

  • A turn longer than one screen becomes readable. This is a correctness bug, not just ergonomics. To read the opponent’s argument the skill snapshots the terminal — but snapshot --viewport returns one screenful, so a rebuttal longer than the viewport is silently truncated, and the alternative (a full snapshot) hands back the entire scrollback to sift. When the turn arrives as a message (or the debater writes its numbered turn-file and messages “turn 03 ready”), the whole argument comes through intact. The file record — the point of the numbered-turn layout — is untouched; the channel just carries the handoff signal.

  • --orchestrate is hub-spoke, and convergence is a subscription. Orchestrate mode (one coordinator shuttling between N ≥ 2 debaters) is hcom’s hub-spoke topology verbatim; today the coordinator round-robin-snapshots each terminal to see who has spoken. Broadcast to @debaters- plus a subscription that fires on the convergence marker (an AGREED in the ledger) turns the manual loop-until-consensus into a reactive one — the exact shape the /codex-debate skill asks for (“no round cap, no deadlock exit — loop until they agree”).

Two honesties keep this from overreaching. The skill’s ban on pulam-tui polling was to keep handoffs direct — agent prompts agent, no middle poller — and the message channel preserves that directness while removing the keystroke fragility; it’s the mechanism the skill already reaches for, done right, not a new indirection. And the borrow is scoped to the terminal debates: the in-process review debates (/lens-debate’s lowy⇄hickey, /codex-debate’s Claude-author subagent) run through the Agent/Workflow tools, never touch a PTY, and so have nothing to screen-scrape and nothing to gain here.

Where kolu keeps its own bet

Two places the comparison says don’t copy hcom — kolu is deliberately, and correctly, different.

  • First-party PTYs, not per-emulator hooks. hcom rides whatever terminal emulator you launched and can close panes on only a subset (kitty, wezterm, tmux, …). kolu owns the terminal through kaval, so it has the live screen, scrollback, resize arbitration, and remote-over-ssh survival as guarantees, not best-effort. This is the herdr bet again, and it’s the reason kolu can render the whole fleet on one canvas instead of N windows.

  • Typed surface-over-ssh, not a shared-PSK MQTT relay. keep This is the sharpest divergence. hcom’s cross-device story is a shared-secret MQTT relay on public brokers, and it is honest about the cost in its own README: “enrollment is total trust — a peer can launch, kill, and drive agents… treat that as shell access on every enrolled device,” with no forward secrecy (a leaked PSK decrypts all past traffic) and no revocation. kolu’s cross-machine path is @kolu/surface-nix-host: nix copy the agent’s closure to a host you already have ssh to, run it over a long-lived ssh subprocess. Per-connection OS auth, no third-party broker, no single secret whose leak is game-over. Adopt hcom’s channel; keep kolu’s transport — the message surface rides mirrorRemoteSurface over that same ssh link, and the MQTT relay never gets built.

  • The fold discipline stays. hcom’s SQLite log is authoritative shared mutable state that every device writes. kolu’s model is a memoryless producer whose events one owner folds into stored state on its own clock (awareness as a fold). A message surface must honor that: messages are occurrences on an Event, folded into an inbox collection by the receiving side — not a shared table many writers mutate. Same capability as hcom, kept inside kolu’s state model rather than importing hcom’s.

The smallest first step

One PR-sized proof, scoped to graduate the one path that doesn’t exist yet — an agent addressing another agent by name over the surface, not by typing into its screen. Concretely: extend the pulam awareness surface with a messages Event and an inbox Collection keyed by agent name; add a kolu-tui msg @name -- <text> verb that publishes to it and a kolu-tui inbox [--wait] that folds and reads it; route delivery through the anyagent adapter as a prompt. The done-criterion that can only be met by hitting the real path: two agents on two different hosts exchange a request/ack pair through the surface — mirrored over ssh by mirrorRemoteSurface, with no keystroke ever injected and no screen ever scraped. Teach it in the kolu skill the same turn, so the loop that today says “send, then snapshot and guess” can instead say “message, then wait for the reply.” Everything above it — subscriptions, collision detection, ensemble scripts — is additive once that channel exists.
